items.question: Why can a single perceptron not learn XOR, but an MLP can?
items.answer: XOR is not linearly separable — no single hyperplane labels all four points correctly. An MLP with a nonlinear hidden layer builds intermediate features (different half-space indicators) that the output neuron combines, producing a piecewise boundary that separates the XOR classes.

items.question: What is the minimum architectural change to turn a linear logistic model into an MLP for tabular data?
items.answer: Insert at least one hidden layer: Linear → nonlinear activation (e.g. ReLU) → output Linear. The hidden tier enables nonlinear feature mixing; the final Linear maps hidden activations to logits or a regression scalar.

items.question: If an MLP uses only Linear layers with no activations, what effective model do you get?
items.answer: One equivalent linear transformation from input to output regardless of how many Linear layers you stack — matrix multiplication associates. Decision boundaries remain linear; depth adds no extra non-linear expressivity.

items.question: How would you design an MLP output head for 10-class classification vs binary classification?
items.answer: Multi-class: final Linear with out_features=10 producing logits, trained with cross-entropy (softmax inside the loss). Binary: one logit with BCEWithLogitsLoss, or two logits with cross-entropy; in both cases hidden layers use ReLU, not softmax.

items.question: Explain how hidden ReLU units create piecewise linear decision boundaries in 2D.
items.answer: Each ReLU hidden unit is active on one half-plane and zero on the other, splitting the plane into regions. The output neuron takes a weighted sum of these region indicators, so the class boundary is a polygonal chain — piecewise linear — rather than a single straight line.
